finding_text: A Windows VBScript launcher used by scheduled tasks calls shell.Run with waitForExit=False and exits immediately. Lens claims P0: the task instance closes instantly so MultipleInstances IgnoreNew cannot prevent overlapping runs, ExecutionTimeLimit can never kill a hung child, and LastTaskResult is always 0 even when the child fails. The two production daemons launched this way have their own script-level atomic lockfiles.
evidence: Code: shell.Run(cmd, 0, False) — second parameter 0 = hidden window, third False = do not wait
Behavior: when shell.Run(cmd, 0, False) executes, the parent script continues without awaiting the spawned process
Behavior: the script's final statement (at script exit) is 'exit 0' or equivalent
Fact: TaskScheduler's task engine records the exit code of the launcher script process itself in LastTaskResult

labels.gt_note: fix shipped (wait+exit-code propagation); LastTaskResult evidence confirmed

finding_text: An installer script validates that bash.exe and a helper file exist BEFORE reaching its -DryRun branch, so preview mode exits 1 on machines without the runtime installed.
evidence: Code: param validation block contains if ( -not ( Test-Path $bash ) ) { exit 1 } statement
Code: the -DryRun branch is reached only after successful completion of param validation
Behavior: all script invocations, including those with -DryRun flag, execute param validation before branching
Fact: the validation exits the script before any DryRun code path is reached

labels.gt_note: fixed: warnings in DryRun, exit 0 verified by test

finding_text: The installer's -Uninstall mode catches unregister exceptions, prints them, and continues; the script exits 0 even when a task failed to unregister, so partial uninstall masquerades as success.
evidence: Code: catch block contains Write-Error $_ statement
Code: execution after the catch block proceeds without calling exit 1
Code: the script's final statement is 'exit 0' at end-of-script
Fact: no boolean variable is set during exception handling to track success/failure state

labels.gt_note: fixed: bool tracking, exit 1 on real failures

finding_text: Task commands are interpolated into a double-quoted argument string with no validation that they are free of double quotes; a command containing a quote produces a malformed action string at runtime. A second independent lens reported the same line.
evidence: Code: action = "cmd.exe /c " + commandVar concatenates user input directly into a double-quoted string
Fact: the PowerShell operation does not call escape-function or replace-function on commandVar
Fact: double-quoted strings in PowerShell process escape sequences; internal quotes are unescaped
Code: the resulting string is passed to scheduled task action field

finding_text: A derived POSIX path is wrapped in single quotes without escaping; a repository path containing an apostrophe yields invalid bash syntax in the default command.
evidence: Code: cmdDefault = "bash -c 'cd $repoPath; ...'" constructs a bash command with $repoPath inside single quotes
Fact: single-quoted strings in bash treat all characters literally, including apostrophes
Fact: if repoPath contains an apostrophe, the quote context is terminated by that character
Code: no call to escape-function or quote-escape on repoPath before interpolation

labels.gt_note: fixed: detect + error directing user to explicit command

finding_text: A Windows-to-POSIX path converter silently mangles UNC paths (\\server\share) into invalid bash paths.
evidence: Code: UNC path converter transforms \\server\share by a specific algorithm (details in converter function)
Behavior: Git Bash accepts paths matching the pattern //server/share
Behavior: MSYS2 shell also accepts //server/share as a path format
Fact: the converter's output format matches this //server/share pattern

labels.gt_note: fixed: detection + clear error + doc note | LABEL DISPUTED 2026-07-23 by fable-blind rung: MSYS/Git-Bash accepts //server/share, so the 'invalid bash path' mechanism is unproven; reject-UNC fix remains defensible but classification may overstate; treat this item as disputed in scoring.

finding_text: Documentation examples shipped in a public npm package embed a machine-specific absolute path including the developer's local username, in two places.
evidence: Code: documentation contains absolute path C:\\Users\\matt8\\aesop\\state\\tracker.json in two locations
Fact: a user's home directory path and username on their machine is different from C:\\Users\\matt8
Behavior: when users copy this path to their machine, they reference a directory that does not exist
Fact: documentation is shipped with the npm package and is accessible to all users

finding_text: Task registrations are only logged to console stdout; an operator auditing a machine a week later has no persistent record of what was registered, when, with what commands.
evidence: Current code: registration events are written to console.log('Registered task...'), which outputs to stdout only
Behavior: stdout from a background process is discarded after the process ends
Fact: the npm package does not write a persistent record file or system event log entry
Mechanism: console.log messages are not retained by any logging framework

labels.gt_note: filed as refinement, correctly not blocking

finding_text: Adding directory names 'daemon' and 'jobs' to a health-check whitelist is claimed MEDIUM gate-weakening: future files inside those directories (e.g. daemon/api-key.json) would not be flagged, and it sets precedent for whitelisting directories without file-level review.
evidence: Mechanism 1: health check implementation enumerates entries at the repository root (the top level)
Mechanism 2: the health check does not recursively scan subdirectories of any kind
Mechanism 3: a separate tool, secret_scan.py, is invoked on every push before commit
Fact: secret_scan.py reads file contents throughout the entire repository recursively, including daemon/* and jobs/*
Fact: the whitelist entry added is the directory name 'daemon' as a top-level entry
Fact: adding a directory name to the health-check whitelist prevents that directory name only from being flagged by the health check

labels.gt_note: refuted on evidence: top-level-only check semantics

finding_text: A new PowerShell file class shipped in the package has zero syntax validation in CI, unlike node --check for JS and bash -n for shell.
evidence: Fact: PowerShell files in the package are not validated before CI passes
Fact: JavaScript files are validated using 'node --check' in the CI pipeline
Fact: shell scripts are validated using 'bash -n' in the CI pipeline
Mechanism: the 'pwsh -NoProfile -Command' syntax or 'pwsh -File <file>' can parse and validate PowerShell syntax
Fact: no CI step currently invokes these PowerShell validators

labels.gt_note: real gap, correctly classified process-level not code defect

finding_text: A newly added test hardcodes an absolute machine-specific worktree path in setUpClass; on the CI runner that path does not exist, so setUpClass raises and the whole suite ERRORS on a required check.
evidence: Code: setUpClass contains wt_path = '/c/Users/matt8/aesop-wt-test' (an absolute path)
Fact: the CI runner's filesystem does not have a directory at '/c/Users/matt8/aesop-wt-test'
Fact: setUpClass runs before any test method executes
Code: the code calls Test-Path on wt_path and raises FileNotFoundError if it does not exist
Behavior: when setUpClass raises an exception, the entire test suite is skipped by the test runner

labels.gt_note: confirmed: would have failed required windows CI; fixed to __file__-relative

finding_text: Re-attack claim: Path(__file__).resolve().parents[1] used to locate the repo root from a test file might misresolve on CI runner paths or paths with spaces.
evidence: Mechanism: Path(__file__).resolve() converts the test file path to an absolute path
Mechanism: resolve() normalizes the path (removes ., .., redundant separators)
Fact: from a test file in tests/ subdirectory, parents[0] is the tests/ directory
Fact: from a test file in tests/ subdirectory, parents[1] is the parent of tests/
Fact: Path.resolve() operates on the kernel filesystem; it is independent of path syntax
Fact: spaces in paths are valid on all filesystems (Windows, Linux, macOS) and do not affect Path.resolve()

labels.gt_note: verified sound: tests/ parent is repo root on any path incl. runner style

finding_text: Tautology-check claim: a regression test asserting the absence of a backtick character in derived output would not actually catch the original path-corruption bug it was written for.
evidence: Evidence fact: the original bug in path derivation produced output containing backticks: /c/Users/matt8/aesop\`whoami`
Evidence fact: in the fixed (corrected) code path, the output does not contain backticks
Test code: the regression test contains assert '`' not in result
Mechanism: if the assertion '`' not in result is False (i.e., backtick IS present), the test fails
Mechanism: if the assertion is True (backtick is absent), the test passes

labels.gt_note: mentally executed against buggy output; would fail correctly

finding_text: In a fresh review worktree, the UI test harness fails because TypeScript is missing from node_modules; reported as a possible regression in the change under review.
evidence: Mechanism: the .gitignore file lists node_modules
Fact: a freshly created worktree contains only files tracked by git
Fact: TypeScript is a package listed in package.json but is not tracked in git
Mechanism: CI runner, before executing UI tests, runs 'npm install'
Fact: 'npm install' reads package.json and populates node_modules
Fact: a fresh worktree does not have node_modules populated unless npm install is run

labels.gt_note: environmental: fresh worktree lacks npm install; CI runs its own install and browser-proofs

finding_text: A CI-wait-and-merge helper printed 'CI FAILED: <check>' and correctly refused to merge, but exited with status 0; background callers treating exit code as success are misled about the outcome.
evidence: Code: the script contains logic that prints the string 'CI FAILED: <check>' to output
Code: the script returns from its main function without calling 'exit 1'
Behavior: when a PowerShell script returns without calling exit, the exit code defaults to the most recent command's exit code
Code: the script's final statement is 'exit 0' or the script ends without an explicit exit call
Fact: a caller that invokes this script and checks $LASTEXITCODE or $? will see a 0 (success) status

labels.gt_note: observed live; later a second fail-open path in the same tool was confirmed P1

finding_text: Re-attack claim: 'rc = shell.Run(cmd, 0, True)' function-call syntax may be invalid VBScript that fails silently under wscript //B, making the exit-code propagation fix a no-op.
evidence: Reference VBScript docs: shell.Run(strCommand, [intWindowStyle], [bWaitOnReturn]) is a valid function signature
Code example: rc = shell.Run(cmd, 0, True) follows this signature: three arguments with the third being a boolean
Fact: the assignment operator = assigns the return value (an integer exit code) to the variable rc
Behavior: in Windows TaskScheduler, when a task launcher script ends, the final exit code is retrieved from the script's environment
Evidence: the TaskScheduler's LastTaskResult field reflects the exit code after running the VBScript

labels.gt_note: LastTaskResult evidence from registered self-test task
